g.data-narration: Welcome to Hypothesis Testing, a fundamental statistical method for making data-driven decisions.
g.text: A Structured Approach to Testing Claims About Population Parameters

g.data-narration: Hypothesis testing is a formal method to validate or reject assumptions about population parameters using sample data.
g.text: A statistical method used to make decisions about a population based on sample data.
It tests whether there is sufficient evidence to support a claim (alternative hypothesis)

g.data-narration: In computer science, hypothesis testing validates algorithms, evaluates A/B tests, and ensures reliable data-driven decisions.
g.text: Why It Matters in Computer Science
A/B Testing: Validate if a new feature improves user engagement
Algorithm Evaluation: Compare performance of ML models
Network Analysis: Test if response times meet performance standards

g.data-narration: Every hypothesis test involves two opposing statements: the null hypothesis assumes no effect, while the alternative challenges it.
g.text: The hypothesis of "no effect" or "no difference"

g.data-narration: The alternative hypothesis determines the test type: left-tailed, right-tailed, or two-tailed based on the direction.
The significance level alpha is the threshold probability of rejecting a true null hypothesis, known as Type I error.
g.text: Probability of making a Type I Error: rejecting H₀ when it is actually true
α = P(Reject H₀ | H₀ is true)
Type I (α): False Positive - reject true H₀
Type II (β): False Negative - fail to reject false H₀

g.data-narration: A test statistic measures how far the sample statistic deviates from the null hypothesis value in standard errors.
g.text: A standardized value calculated from sample data that measures deviation from H₀
Where: x̄ = sample mean, μ₀ = hypothesized mean, s = sample std dev, n = sample size

g.data-narration: The p-value is the probability of observing results at least as extreme as the sample, assuming the null hypothesis is true.
g.text: The probability of obtaining a test statistic at least as extreme as observed, assuming H₀ is true.
Small p-value (≤ α): Strong evidence against H₀ → Reject H₀
Large p-value (> α): Weak evidence against H₀ → Fail to Reject H₀

g.data-narration: The decision rule is simple: reject the null hypothesis if the p-value is less than or equal to the significance level.
Let's apply these concepts to a real scenario: testing if a new algorithm reduces average page load time.
g.text: Scenario: New algorithm claims to reduce average page load time.
Historical average: μ₀ = 3.2 seconds | Sample: n = 25, x̄ = 2.9s, s = 0.5s

g.data-narration: Using the t-formula, we calculate t equals negative three point zero, indicating the sample mean is three standard errors below the hypothesis.
g.text: H₁: μ < 3.2 seconds (Reduction claim) - Left-tailed test

g.data-narration: For a left-tailed t-test with t equals negative three and twenty-four degrees of freedom, the p-value is approximately zero point zero zero three.
g.text: For a left-tailed t-test with t = -3.0 and df = 24:
Using t-distribution table or software: p-value = 0.003

g.data-narration: Since the p-value zero point zero zero three is less than alpha zero point zero five, we reject the null hypothesis.
In summary, hypothesis testing involves formulating null and alternative hypotheses, calculating a test statistic, and comparing the p-value to the significance level.
Remember: rejecting the null hypothesis does not prove the alternative is true; it only provides statistical evidence in its favor.
g.text: Important Note: "Rejecting H₀" ≠ "Proving H₁ is true" — It means the sample provides strong evidence in favor of H₁.
